domain: Browser-based educational block-coding puzzle game with telemetry-enabled analytics.
constraints: Provide landing, level-select, and 3 sequential puzzles teaching sequencing, loops, and conditionals.
Each puzzle uses a left library/right code area with fixed `On Start` root, warning for disconnected blocks, and visible Play/Reset/speed toggle controls.
Scenes must adopt provided assets/layout guidance: horizontal alignment, obstacle midpoint, assets sourced from `public/assets/...` and layout references from `design/layout_refs/`.
Failure handling must show `Oops!` plus hint linked to failure reason covering target miss, wrong item, order, obstacle collision, missing condition.
Workspace must hide/minimize during execution, offer command categories (movement, actions, control/loops, logic/conditionals, sensing) and a Show Code text view.

Telemetry must capture detailed gameplay/UI events (sessions, puzzles, attempts, events, movements, block execution, collisions, pickups, hints, outcomes) persisted in SQLite with schema tables defined in brief.
Backend API must expose `POST /api/session/start`, `POST /api/session/end`, `POST /api/events/batch`, and `GET /api/analytics/*` for ingestion/analytics.

Visual identity: polished cartoon style in pink/violet palette, WebGL-based 2D rendering with smooth animation, keyboard-accessible controls, sufficient contrast, optional text-to-speech for goal text.
success_criteria: Exactly 3 playable puzzles with progression and clear goals succeed/fail properly.
Every puzzle run animates via Play and supports Reset plus failure hints.
SQLite auto-creates; successful run stores attempts/events/movements rows.
Analytics UI can replay runs using stored movement data.
Workspace enforces `On Start` root with proper block categories and warnings.
Telemetry coverage includes all required gameplay/UI events without dropping movement steps.
expected_outputs: Next.js 16 TypeScript app combining frontend puzzle UI, block editor, and backend API routes.
Puzzle data definitions (id, story, goal, scene, grid, entities, available blocks, constraints, success criteria, hints).
SQLite schema/init logic (users, sessions, puzzles, attempts, events, movements, puzzle_progress) using better-sqlite3.
Frontend telemetry emitters plus backend ingestion endpoints and analytics dashboards (aggregates, puzzle detail, attempt replay).
Scene setups leveraging provided sprites/backgrounds with animation logic and hints tied to failure states.
stack_requirements: Next.js 16.x with TypeScript for both frontend and backend routes.
SQLite persistence via better-sqlite3 (latest node-v24 compatible).
WebGL-capable 2D rendering in browser for scenes/animations.
Drag-and-drop block editor (custom or Blockly) heavily restyled to match art direction.
validation_expectations: Run build/test commands suited to Next.js project to confirm compilation.
Start dev server ensuring automatic SQLite creation and DB migrations/seed run.
Trigger gameplay runs to verify telemetry rows for attempts/events/movements and failure hints.
Use analytics pages to read stored data and replay movement paths from DB.
repo_constraints: Use local asset folders (`public/assets/backgrounds`, `public/assets/sprites`, `design/layout_refs`) as canonical sources; no external IP.
Adhere to supplied first-level layout reference when composing scene; extrapolate for later puzzles without deviating from core arrangement logic.
No destructive git commands or assumption of hidden requirements; rely solely on repository artifacts.
missing_information: Exact puzzle narratives beyond initial layout reference and concept focus are unspecified.
Detailed telemetry payload schemas (field names, types) are not provided.
Specific animation engine or WebGL library choice is left open.
Accessibility specifics for text-to-speech implementation (voices, API) are undefined.
assumptions: Design team will accept custom stylized scenes derived from provided assets/layout for all puzzles.
Telemetry schema can be derived from named tables/requirements without extra columns beyond reasonable inference.
A custom block editor or themed Blockly skin is acceptable if it satisfies styling and functional requirements.
